furancarbonilglicina.name: Furan carbonyl glycine
furancarbonilglicina.base: Furan carbonyl glycine is a nonspecific metabolite produced by Aspergillus and other fungal species in the gastrointestinal tract. Aspergillus transmits opportunistic mycoses: similarly, to Candida albicans, Aspergillus creates damage in the host (man) exclusively under conditions favorable to it. In fact, Aspergillus is among the commensal microorganisms that, only in some precise circumstances, turn into opportunistic pathogens. The decline in the host's immune defenses, asthma and bronchial cysts are important predisposing factors. The most common source of exposure is moldy food or household mold.
furancarbonilglicina.hight: Excess furan carbonyl glycine identifies fungal overgrowth, so it is advisable to investigate dietary habits and possible environmental exposures. Then evaluate an anti-fungal therapy and perform a specific test for fungal infections.

acidotricarballilico.name: Tricarballyl acid
acidotricarballilico.base: Tricarballyl acid is a chemical byproduct released by fumonisins (contaminant of corn products) as it passes through the gastrointestinal tract. Fumonisins are fungal toxins produced by the fungus Fusarium verticillioides. Tricarballyl acid is an inhibitor of the enzyme aconitase, magnesium chelator, and therefore interferes with the Krebs cycle. In addition, trycarballyl acid is a by-product of refining beet sugar and maple sugar and may appear after ingestion of these sugars.
acidotricarballilico.hight: Excess tricarballyl acid could identify fungal overgrowth. Clinical syndromes due to intact mycotoxin are rare and characterized by abdominal pain and diarrhea. It can also appear after consumption of sugar beet or maple syrup.

arabinosio.name: Arabinose
arabinosio.base: Arabinose is a pentose sugar product of degradation of hyaluronic acid, it can also be found in some fruits (apples, plums, cherries and grapes). Arabinose is found in all organisms, from bacteria to plants to animals. There are two different paths of use of arabinose in nature: bacterial and fungal. The bacterial pathway converts arabinose to xylulose-5-P after which it enters the pentose phosphate pathway for ethanol production. The fungal pathway converts arabinose to L-arabinitol and eventually enters the non-oxidative pentose phosphate pathway for further metabolism. The presence of arabinose in the urine may indicate an overgrowth of intestinal yeast such as Candida albicans or other yeast/fungal species. L-arabinose is also a microbial metabolite found and produced by Mycobacterium. Candidiasis is an opportunistic infection caused by yeast and can lead to various changes: dysbiosis, food allergies and intolerances, oxidative stress, etc.
arabinosio.hight: Excess arabinose could indicate ongoing infection with Candida or other yeasts. Also evaluate the eating style of the patient in case of important presence of this metabolite, reduce sugars. It could also be a marker of an important use of fruit.

arabitolo.name: Arabitol
arabitolo.base: Arabitol is polyol (sugar alcohol) obtained from the reduction of arabinose. It is considered a marker of invasive candidiasis or infection with Debaryomyces, Pichia and Zygosaccharomyces.
arabitolo.hight: Excess arabitol in urine identifies ongoing fungal infection. It has also been associated with Alzheimer's disease. Metabolic inborn errors can cause it to increase in urine.

acido25furandicarbossilico.name: 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid
acido25furandicarbossilico.base: 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid is a normal urinary metabolite in humans, resulting from dietary use of cooked meat. 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid is also a nonspecific microbial metabolite and biomarker of high amounts of fructose.
acido25furandicarbossilico.hight: Excess 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid is associated with nonspecific fungal growth or strong fruit intake.

acido4idrossiippurico.name: 4-Hydroxyhippuric acid
acido4idrossiippurico.base: 4-hydroxyhippuric acid is a microbial product derived from the metabolism of polyphenols by the intestinal microflora or of food/pharmacological origin for the intake of polyphenols or parabens.
acido4idrossiippurico.hight: The excess of 4-hydroxyhippuric acid can highlight the individual's exposure to parabens found in foods, cosmetics and most pharmaceuticals. Although they have been defined in the past as safe for humans, their low toxicity continues to be debated. The excess of this metabolite can also be associated with the intake of fruits containing polyphenols rich in anthocyanins, flavonols and hydroxycinnamates, or with an excess of bacteria that metabolize polyphenols.

acidoippurico.name: Hippuric acid
acidoippurico.base: Hippuric acid is derived from benzoic acid conjugated with glycine in the liver (Benzoate + Glycine -> Hippurate). Most of the hippuric acid in urine is derived from the microbial degradation of chlorogenic acid into benzoic acid. Chlorogenic acid is a substance commonly found in beverages (tea, coffee) and fruits (apples, pears, blueberries, carrots, tomatoes, peaches) but also in sunflower seeds, potatoes, eggplants and sweet potatoes. Benzoic acid is present in large quantities in cranberry juice and is a food preservative. Hippuric acid can also be generated due to prolonged exposure to the chemical solvent toluene. In general, it is more representative as a marker of the metabolism of bacterial phenolic compounds.
acidoippurico.hight: The excess of hippuric acid is caused by the bacterial metabolism of phenolic compounds introduced into the usual diet. More rarely it can be associated with hydrocarbon poisoning.
acidoippurico.low: Low levels of hippuric acid are associated with dysbiosis, to be investigated by specific test.

acidobenzoico.name: Benzoic acid
acidobenzoico.base: Benzoic acid is a byproduct of phenylalanine metabolism in bacteria. It is also produced when intestinal bacteria (E. coli, Bifids, Lactobacilli, Eubacterium, Bacteroidetes) process polyphenols (from fruits, coffee, tea or vegetables). It can be found in the infectious agent Serratia.
acidobenzoico.hight: Its excess can also be related to cystic fibrosis, enteritis, celiac disorder, short bowel syndrome or massive intake of food preservatives.

indacano.name: Indican
indacano.base: Indican is a colorless organic compound consisting of an indole ring conjugated to glucose. It is a substance naturally present in the urine of humans and mammals in general, as a metabolite of tryptophan. Tryptophan is converted into indole by gut bacteria, particularly by (e.g., Pepto streptococcus indolicus, Bacteroides putredinis and Clostridium sporogeneses) which metabolize it allowing the loss of the side chain; the indole produced is then absorbed by the intestinal mucosa entering the entero-hepatic circulation. In the liver it undergoes an enzymatic process of detoxification biphasic: in the first step it is transformed into 3-hydroxyindole and subsequently conjugated with sulfuric acid or glucuronic acid for its excretion through the kidneys and urine in the form of indican. The urinary concentration of indigo reflects the presence of putrefactive phenomena affecting proteins and nitrogen compounds, a process mediated by some bacterial species such as Proteus and Klebsiella.  The fundamental action of these bacteria allows to carry out the first step of detoxification of the compoundswith the formation of endotoxic substances (ammonia, box, indole, phenols) which subsequently reach the liver compromising its functionality.  The indole, along with the box, is responsible for much of the smell of feces.
indacano.hight: The excessive presence of indican is related to dysbiosis that can be mild (around 20 mg / l), medium (if they investigate it is between 20 and 40 mg / l) or severe (if greater than 40 mg / l).    Also evaluate the nutritional status as a diet rich in protein induces an increase in this metabolite. A deficiency of Lactobacilli bacteria contributes to the patient's lack of eubiosis, causing symptoms such as bloating, digestive difficulties, fatigue and immune weakness. Other causes could be metabolic diseases such as blue diaper syndrome or Hartnup's disease.

acido4idrossifenilacetico.name: 4-Hydroxyphenylacetic acid
acido4idrossifenilacetico.base: 4-Hydroxyphenylacetic acid is a microbial metabolite produced by Acinetobacter, Clostridium, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas and Proteus. This metabolite is produced from Tyrosine and can be converted into Cresol. Important food sources such as soy, turkey, chicken, almonds, pumpkin seeds make this metabolite biomarker of the use of these foods.
acido4idrossifenilacetico.hight: Excess of this metabolite is associated with the overgrowth of small gut bacteria from Clostridia species including C. difficile, C.  stricklandii, C.  lituseburense, C.  subterminal, C.  putrefaciens and C.  propionicum. Giardia Lamblia infection, small intestine diseases, use of antibiotics against aerobic bacteria, cystic fibrosis, transient lactose intolerance can also induce an increase in this metabolite.

acido3indoloacetico.name: 3-indole acetic acid
acido3indoloacetico.base: 3-indole acetic acid is a metabolite derived from tryptophan by intestinal putrefaction. Its production derives from E. coli, P. vulgaris, A. faciens and Aerogenes, in some cases also from Clostridia.
acido3indoloacetico.hight: The excess of 3-indoleacetic (and its intermediate box) could be induced by a diet rich in meat. Excessive production is a sign of toxicity of the organism caused by dysbiosis or an unbalanced diet. Very high amounts of this tryptophan-derived metabolite could indicate a depletion of tryptophan necessary for other physiological functions.

pcresolo.name: P-cresol
pcresolo.base: P-cresol is a metabolite of aromatic amino acids produced by the intestinal microflora. Tyrosine and phenylalanine are first converted to 4-hydroxyphenylacetic acid, then this is decarboxylated to p-cresol (putrefaction).  This role is played both by aerobic bacteria (enterobacteria), but also by anaerobes (Clostridium). Clostrium difficile is a bacterium often widespread in hospitals, capable of causing increases in cresol production in affected subjects. C. difficile strains produce toxins that trigger diarrhea, colitis and complications of these until death in the most debilitated individuals. Excessive amounts of p-cresol can inhibit the enzyme dopamine-beta-hydroxylase, leading to neurotransmitter imbalances. High amounts of p-cresol have been found in autistic subjects.
pcresolo.hight: In uremia, changes in the intestinal flora determine the specific overgrowth of bacteria that are specific producers of p-cresol. Excess protein in the diet results in increased secretion of this metabolite. Kidney diseases also show increased p-cresol.

3idrossifenilacetico.name: 3-Hydroxyphenylacetic acid
3idrossifenilacetico.base: 3-hydroxyphenylacetic acid is produced by amino acid fermentation (purely tyrosine) caused by poor digestion and absorption. It is often used as a marker for protein malabsorption. Also, the intake of food polyphenols (wine, green tea, grapes) causes the appearance of this metabolite in the urine. Its presence in the urine also depends on infection caused by Clostridia or Klebsiella.
3idrossifenilacetico.hight: While in healthy subjects these residues are limited, they can be numerous in phenylketonuria, a rare metabolic disease. It can also be a marker of the use of foods with polyphenols, protein malabsorption and strong dysbiosis caused by Clostridia or Klebsiella.

HPHPA.name: HPHPA
HPHPA.base: HPHPA stands for 3-(3-hydroxyphenyl)-3-hydroxypropionic acid. It is abundant in adult human urine and is benign in small amounts. The source of this compound is mostly from anaerobic bacteria of the genus Clostridium. Clostridia can significantly alter brain neurotransmitters, inactivating dopamine beta-hydroxylase leading to excessive dopamine production and reduced norepinephrine. Excess dopamine is associated with forms of psychotic disorder. If this marker is high, it is useful to relate it with the main metabolite of dopamine (homovanyllic acid) and with that of norepinephrine (vanilmandelic acid).
HPHPA.hight: Excess HPHPA indicates excessive growth of Clostridia bacteria in the gastrointestinal tract. When chronically high, the impact found in humans is often related to schizophrenia and autism spectrum, in fact this metabolite is communally elevated in GAPS cases (Gut Syndrome and Psychology).

acidoglicolico.name: Glycolic acid
acidoglicolico.base: Glycolic acid is the smallest alpha-hydroxy acid. It is very high in cases of genetic hyperoxaluria type I. Normal glycolic acid values exclude the genetic cause of significant increase in oxalic acid in the urine.  The renal toxicity of glycolic acid is due to its metabolism with oxalic acid. These two acids, together with lactic, are responsible for anionic gap metabolic acidosis. Glycolic acid is also present in food (glycerol or glycerin), but more often it is produced by yeasts (Aspergillo, Penicillo or Candida) or gastrointestinal bacteria (Acetobacteria, Corinebacteria, Escherichia ...).
acidoglicolico.hight: The excess of glycolic acid in association with the increase of oxalic acid, defines genetic hyperoxaluria type I. Otherwise its excessive presence can be of food origin or derived from bacterial production or intestinal yeasts.

acidoglicerico.name: Glyceric acid
acidoglicerico.base: Glyceric acid can be a marker of genetic pathology D-glycerine aciduria, acting as a metabotoxin and acidogen. Otherwise, glyceric acid can derive from microbial sources such as yeasts (Aspergillo, Penicillo or Candida) or from food sources of glycerol or glycerin.
acidoglicerico.hight: Marked excess glyceric acid can reveal D-glyceric aciduria. Otherwise, its excessive presence can be of food origin or derived from intestinal yeasts.

acidoossalico.name: Oxalic acid
acidoossalico.base: Oxalic acid is a strong dicarboxylic acid produced by the human body by the metabolism of glyoxyc acid or ascorbic acid. It is known primarily for its role in the formation of kidney stones in association with calcium. Although the dietary contribution of green leafy vegetables, fresh fruit and dried fruit can affect the formation of calcium oxalates, these also have a protective effect, so it is good to make a limited use of them. Even the integration of ascorbic acid, precursor of oxalic, could favor the formation of kidney stones so it is recommended a minimum integration for subjects predisposed to stones. The major producers of oxalic acid are some species of Aspergillus, Penicillium and Candida, so ad hoc antifungal therapy is advisable, administration of lactic acid bacteria and probiotic agents. Genetic predispositions for deficiency of the enzyme fumarase and primary hyperoxaluria I or peroxisome deficiency can cause a major increase in oxalates resulting in damage to skin, blood vessels and bone tissues. In these cases, maintaining high levels of magnesium and urinary citrate, you can help the expulsion of stones and limit oxalate deposition diseases.
acidoossalico.hight: Excess oxalic acid could be associated with major vitamin C supplementation or fungal infection and dysbiosis. If the other metabolites of oxalates (  glycolic  acid and glyceric acid) are also elevated, it is likely that it is a genetic predisposition to hyperoxaluria; otherwise you can act by treating specifically the intestinal bacterial flora and limit the intake of oxalates and vit.C. Evaluate magnesium and citrate supplementation to promote the expulsion of kidney stones.

acidopantotenico.name: Pantothenic acid
acidopantotenico.base: Pantothenic acid refers to vit. B5, water-soluble vitamin necessary to form coenzyme-A, fundamental for the metabolism and synthesis of carbohydrates, proteins and fats. It has an important role in regulating blood cholesterol, maintaining the nervous system and hormone levels. Food sources of pantothenic acid are meat, eggs, whole grain products, legumes, milk, fruits and vegetables. It is usually released quickly in the urine.
acidopantotenico.hight: Excess is associated with ongoing or past supplementation of vit. B5.
acidopantotenico.low: Poor pantothenic acid values may reflect vitamin B5 deficiency, for which it is advisable to review the patient's usual diet and evaluate specific supplementation.

acidoglutarico.name: Glutaric acid
acidoglutarico.base: Glutaric acid is an intermediate product of the metabolism of certain amino acids, including lysine and tryptophan. The increase indicates deficiency of riboflavin (vitamin B2), cofactor needed by the enzyme glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase that is supposed to convert glutaric acid to Cotonyl-CoA. Other factors that increase glutaric acid levels include fatty acid oxidation defects and metabolic effects of valproic acid (Depakene) or celiac disease.
acidoglutarico.hight: Excess glutaric acid could represent vitamin deficiency. B2 or oxidation defects. Very high values of glutaric acid highlight the genetic disease glutaric acidemia type I, in which the enzyme that is supposed to mediate amino acid catabolism does not work properly.

acidometilmalonico.name: Methylmalonic acid
acidometilmalonico.base: Methylmalonic acid is a metabolite formed from propionyl-CoA derived from valine, isoleucine, methionine, threonine and odd-chain fatty acids then converted to methylmalonyl-CoA. Methylmalonyl-CoA has two destinies: the first is to enter the Krebs cycle as succinyl-CoA, through the action of methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, an enzyme that uses vit. B12 as a cofactor, the second destiny is instead to be converted into methylmalonic acid and then eliminated through the urine.
acidometilmalonico.hight: Excess methylmalonic acid reflects vit. B12 mainly but can also detect a rare enzyme deficiency of methylmalonyl-CoA mutase. The deficiency of vit. B12 could be caused by intestinal absorption problems, alcoholism and a strictly vegan diet. Food sources of vitamin B12 are of animal origin: liver, milk and dairy products, beef and eggs. The level of methylmalonic acid appears to be a more sensitive indicator of vitamin B12 deficiency than the quantification of vitamin B12 directly in serum. It is important to monitor this metabolite in the prevention of cognitive decline and neuropathy.

nacetilcisteina.name: N-acetylcysteine
nacetilcisteina.base: N-acetylcysteine is a powerful antioxidant that increases glutathione stores in the body. Together with glutathione, of which it is a precursor, N-acetylcysteine binds directly to toxic metabolites, also carrying out a cytoprotective action for the cell. Although N-acetylcysteine may be helpful under certain conditions, overuse of the supplement could be harmful. NAC supplementation is often used in cases of paracetamol overdose, in which N-acetylcysteine acts to maintain or replenish depleted glutathione stores in the liver and improve non-toxic metabolism of paracetamol. NAC is also used as a mucolytic or antiviral agent in patients with HIV.
nacetilcisteina.hight: Excess N-acetylcysteine is due to ongoing or past supplementation.

acidoascorbico.name: Ascorbic acid
acidoascorbico.base: Ascorbic acid occurs naturally in fruits and vegetables and is an essential nutrient in the human diet. The biologically active form of ascorbic acid is vitamin C. The human body is not able to synthesize it independently, so it is essential to introduce it through food. Food sources of vitamin C are kiwi, strawberries, citrus fruits, pineapples, vegetables and fresh vegetables. Vit. C functions as a reducing agent and coenzyme of several metabolic pathways (carnitine and norepinephrine biosynthesis, tyrosine metabolism, collagen hydroxylation and peptide hormone amidation). Its ability to donate electrons makes it a powerful water-soluble antioxidant that readily eliminates free radicals such as molecular oxygen, superoxide, hydroxyl radical and hypochlorous acid. In this context, several mechanisms could explain a link between vit. C and heart disease, primarily the relationship between the oxidation of LDL cholesterol and vit. C and E, as well as the mechanisms that improve vasodilation and vascular reactivity.
acidoascorbico.hight: Excess in urine is related to vit supplementation. C. Excess ascorbic acid may also be associated with hyperoxaemia.
acidoascorbico.low: Its deficit is associated with deficiency. Vitamin C supplementation is advisable in these cases.

acido4piridossico.name: 4-Pyridoxic acid
acido4piridossico.base: 4-Pyridoxic acid is a member of the class of compounds known as methylpyridines and is the catabolic product of vitamin B6. Vitamin B6 intervenes in the process of transformation of proteins, carbohydrates and fats and contributes to the formation of hemoglobin. It is also important for normal brain development and the immune system. It is also essential for the synthesis of serotonin, which together with other neurotransmitters helps regulate mood, sleep, appetite, memory and the ability to concentrate. Because some individuals may require very high doses of vitamin B6, high values do not indicate the need to reduce vitamin B6 intake. Food sources of vitamin B6 are meat, fish, liver, whole grains, eggs, milk and cheese, but also chickpeas, lentils and dried fruit. 4-pyridoxyc acid deficiency in urine may be related to poor vitamin levels. B6, high oxalates, low neurotransmitters or malabsorption problems. Evaluate vitamin B6 supplementation.
acido4piridossico.hight: Very high values are associated with past or ongoing supplementation of B vitamins.
acido4piridossico.low: Urine 4-pyridoxic acid deficiency could be related to poor levels of vit. B6, high oxalates, low neurotransmitters or malabsorption problems. Evaluate vitamin B6 supplementation.

3idrossi3metilglutarico.name: 3-Hydroxy-3-methylglutaric acid (HMG)
3idrossi3metilglutarico.base: 3-Hydroxy-3-methylglutaric acid (HMG) is an intermediate in the degradation process of leucine, but also a metabolic precursor of cholesterol and CoQ10. 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA is usually converted to acetyl-CoA and acetoacetate by the enzyme 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA lyase but in case of enzyme inefficiency there is no such conversion with accumulation of HMG leading to mitochondrial toxicity. The enzyme HMG-CoA lyase also plays an important role in the formation of the ketone body 3-hydroxybutyric acid. HMG is a metabolite with lipid-lowering action and inhibits cholesterol synthesis. Drugs that interfere with the HMG-CoA lyase enzyme induce high levels of HMG and consequent low levels of cholesterol and CoQ10.
3idrossi3metilglutarico.hight: Values above the reference range may be found in patients taking statins and red yeast rice, for which CoQ10 supplementation is recommended. Deficiency of the HMG-CoA lyase enzyme can elevate HMG levels, leading to CoQ10 deficiency and developmental disorders and cardiomyopathies.
3idrossi3metilglutarico.low: The shortage does not see relevant clinical associations.

uracile.name: Uracil
uracile.base: Uracil and thymine are related to folic acid metabolism. Folic acid is fundamental for the replication of gene material as it plays a key role in the biosynthesis of purines and pyrimidines (constituents of DNA and RNA) as well as amino acids: in humans it is assimilated by the diet and not synthesized. Folic acid deficiency is negatively implicated in these processes of genomic or epigenetic regulation. The enzyme 5,10-methyltetridrofolate reductase (5,10-MTHFR) is a folic acid-dependent enzyme that exists in polymorphic form. The action of the enzyme 5,10-MTHFR regulates both DNA phenotypic expression and homocysteine metabolism. In the face of a correct nutritional intake of folic acid, the optimal presence of 5,10-MTHFR protects against the risk of incorrect embryogenetic development as well as from the development of many degenerative diseases. Uracyl and thymine are released from RNA and DNA, with increased genomic instability and epigenetic effects, in the case of folic acid deficiency.
uracile.hight: Excess of one or both metabolites uracil and thymine is related to disorders of folic acid metabolism, folic acid deficiency and genetic disorders of pyrimidine metabolism. A low intake of folic acid and the consequent reduced production of the enzyme 5,10-MTHFR that intervenes in the correct conversion from dUMP (deoxy uridine monophosphate) to dTMP (deoxy thymidine monophosphate), determines an increase in the mis-incorporation of uracil in DNA with an increase in genomic instability. Research shows that the processes involved in programmed cell death are closely correlated with the presence of circulating folic acid which is responsible for the integrity of the human genome.

timina.base: Uracil and thymine are related to folic acid metabolism. Folic acid is fundamental for the replication of gene material as it plays a key role in the biosynthesis of purines and pyrimidines (constituents of DNA and RNA) as well as amino acids: in humans it is assimilated by the diet and not synthesized. Folic acid deficiency is negatively implicated in these processes of genomic or epigenetic regulation. The action of the enzyme 5,10-MTHFR regulates both DNA phenotypic expression and homocysteine metabolism. In the face of a correct nutritional intake of folic acid, the optimal presence of 5,10-MTHFR protects against the risk of incorrect embryogenetic development as well as from the development of many degenerative diseases. 2idrossiippurico.name: 2-Hydroxyhippuric acid
2idrossiippurico.base: 2-Hydroxyhippuric acid (salicyluric acid) is a conjugate of the amino acid glycine and benzoic acid. It is formed by the body to eliminate excess salicylates resulting from the use of aspirin or the consumption of aspartame-based sweeteners. 2-hydroxyhippuric acid can also result from the conversion of tyrosine and phenylalanine by gastrointestinal bacteria. Very high rates inhibit dopamine beta-hydroxylase with elevation of HVA (homovanillic acid) and reduction of VMA (vanylmandelic acid).
2idrossiippurico.hight: The increase in urine may also be related to the growth of gastrointestinal bacteria, the use of artificial sweeteners containing aspartame or excessive use of aspirin.

acidoorotico.name: Orotic acid
acidoorotico.base: Orotic acid is an intermediate product in nucleotide synthesis and is linked to arginine metabolism as a marker of ammonium balance in the urea cycle. If there is an insufficient detoxification capacity of ammonium by urea cycle, the synthesis of orotate takes place.
acidoorotico.hight: The increase in urine of this metabolite is representative of an arginine deficiency and simultaneously of a decrease in the efficiency of detoxifying ammonia and a reduced formation of nitric oxide. Innate errors in metabolism can increase orotate and ammonia levels, or it can result from excessive intestinal bacterial growth. Even a strong use of dairy products and cow's milk induces elevated orotate in the urine, excessive use of protein in the diet or alcohol abuse. Drugs such as allopurinol and 6-azauridine seem to be correlated with the synthesis of pyrimidine and therefore orotate. Evaluate supplementation with arginine and magnesium.

acidopiroglutammico.name: Pyroglutamic acid
acidopiroglutammico.base: Pyroglutamic acid, also called 5-oxoproline, is produced and used by GGT (gamma-glutamyl transferase) for the production and recycling of glutathione, a fundamental antioxidant. Glutathione is a tripeptide formed by glutamine, glycine and cysteine. Pyroglutamate is a precursor of glutathione through which the union of the three amino acids can take place. This action is mediated by cofactors such as magnesium and ATP. Under physiological conditions, a modest amount of pyroglutamate is present in the urine.
acidopiroglutammico.hight: The pathological conditions in which an increase in pyroglutamate occurs in the  urine are due to the alteration of one or more enzymes that collaborate in the cycle for its synthesis or mitochondrial dysfunction due to oxidative stress or to food causes (glycine, cysteine, glutamine and magnesium restriction or taurine supplementation) or pharmacological (drugs that require conjugation with glutathione). Glutathione deficiency conditions, e.g., due to acetomifen toxicity, also lead to elevated pyroglutamate levels. Evaluate supplementation with glutathione, NAC and amino acids (methionine and glycine).
acidopiroglutammico.low: The deficiency shows a lack of glutathione or cofactors necessary for its synthesis (glutamine, glycine and cysteine).

acido2idrossibutirrico.name: 2-Hydroxybutyric acid
acido2idrossibutirrico.base: 2-hydroxybutyric acid is a product of mammalian tissues (hepatic) that catabolize L-threonine or synthesize glutathione; therefore, it is considered a marker of oxidative stress. 2-hydroxybutyric acid is an organic acid produced from alpha-ketobutyrate (byproduct in the glutathione synthesis pathway) through the enzymes lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) or alpha-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase (HBDH), enzymes catalyzed by NADH. In case of metabolic stress, it is essential to have important supplies of L-cysteine for the synthesis of glutathione, so homocysteine is diverted to the transolforation pathway forming cystathionine. From the cleavage of cystathionine into cysteine, alpha-ketobutyrate is formed and then 2-hydroxybutyric acid. Oxidative stress induced by insulin resistance also increases the rate of hepatic synthesis of glutathione, therefore it could be used as a biomarker of prediabetic stages.
acido2idrossibutirrico.hight: Elevated levels of 2-hydroxybutyric acid are associated with conditions in which hepatic synthesis of glutathione from methionine is increased due to oxidative stress or detoxification functions. Conditions of poor diet, smoking, lack of exercise inhibit the action of alpha-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase (HBDH) which catalyzes the conversion of 2-hydroxybutyrate into acetate, thus causing an increase in the urine. It can be associated with prediabetic stages or is found high in infectious diseases such as measles. In case of birth asphyxia or inherited metabolic diseases, it may appear elevated.

acido4metil2ossovalerico.name: 4-methyl-2-oxovaric acid
acido4metil2ossovalerico.base: 4-methyl-2-oxovaric acid, also called ketoleucine, is an abnormal metabolite that results from the incomplete breakdown of branched-chain amino acids. Isoleucine, leucine and valine are three essential branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs) that are taken using proteins or sports supplements, providing ready-to-use energy. Skeletal muscle catabolizes these BCAA amino acids via aminotransferase enzymes to form keto acids in the blood so that they are then metabolized later by the liver. BCAAs are substrates for protein synthesis, energy production, neurotransmitter production and other metabolic processes. Keto acids require an enzyme complex called branched-chain alpha-keto dehydrogenase (BCKD) to be metabolized, an enzyme complex that requires vitamin cofactors such as vit. B1, B2, B3, B5 and lipoic acid. If keto acids are not metabolized, they accumulate becoming neurotoxic and metabotoxic. A neurotoxin causes damage to nerve cells and nerve tissues. A metabotoxin is an endogenous metabolite that causes adverse health effects at chronically high levels. Chronically elevated levels of ketoleucine are associated with maple syrup urine disease, a metabolic disorder caused by a deficiency of the branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase complex (BCKD) leading to the accumulation of branched-chain amino acids (leucine, isoleucine and valine) and their toxic byproducts (keto acids) in the blood and urine.
acido4metil2ossovalerico.hight: Excess is rare and associated with alkaptonuria. If the value is slightly altered, supplement the enzyme cofactor iron and the antioxidant vit. C, then check back over time by investigating cardiac, renal and joint functions. Evaluate a low-protein diet.

acidomandelico.name: Mandelic acid
acidomandelico.base: Mandelic acid is a derivative of phenylalanine and of the metabolism of adrenaline and noradrenaline. It can also be produced through exposure to styrene, a toxic environmental compound. It also has antibacterial and soothing properties, which is why it is often used in cosmetics.
acidomandelico.hight: An excess of mandelic acid is often related to phenylalanine metabolism or exposure to toxic substances. It may also be elevated in cases of genetic disorders such as PKU and tyrosinemia.

acidoomogentisico.name: Homogentisic acid
acidoomogentisico.base: Homogentisic acid, also known as melanic acid, is a metabolite involved in the degradation of tyrosine and phenylalanine, mediated by the enzyme homogentisate oxidase and the iron cofactor. If the enzyme is mutated, an excess of homogentisic acid occurs along with the clinical signs of alkaptonuria, a rare metabolic disorder: darkening of urine, arthritis, kidney stones, heart valve disease, etc. It may also be considered a potential marker of coronary risk related to oxidative stress, as well as an osteotoxin and nephrotoxin, causing damage to bones, joints, and/or kidneys. In fact, the accumulation of homogentisic acid leads to damage to cartilage and heart valves, and may precipitate as kidney stones. The conversion of homogentisic acid into benzoquinone acetic acid leads to the formation of polymers that deposit in cartilage collagen, causing ochronosis and subsequently osteoarthritis.
acidoomogentisico.hight: Excess levels are rare and generally associated with alkaptonuria. If the value is slightly altered, it is advisable to supplement the enzymatic cofactor iron and the antioxidant vitamin C, then monitor over time while assessing cardiac, renal, and joint function. A low-protein diet may also be considered.

acido2idrossiisocaproico.name: 2-Hydroxyisocaproic acid
acido2idrossiisocaproico.base: 2-hydroxyisocaproic acid is a product of leucine metabolism. It is widely used by the body building community. From recent studies, it seems proven that in patients with maple syrup disease accelerates lipid peroxidation. 2-Hydroxyisocaproic acid is also produced by Lactobacilli and other fungal species.
acido2idrossiisocaproico.hight: Moderate excess of 2-hydroxyisocaproic acid is associated with leucine supplementation or fungal overgrowth. If in strong excess, investigate whether it is maple syrup disease (MSUD), an inherited metabolic disorder characterized by neurological dysfunction, psychomotor and mental retardation.

acidoNacetilaspartico.name: N-acetyl aspartic acid
acidoNacetilaspartico.base: N-acetyl aspartic acid is a derivative of aspartic acid, an amino acid present in sweeteners with aspartame, supplements with aspartic acid or derived salts or in plant and animal sources to a lesser extent. N-acetyl aspartic acid is the second most concentrated molecule in the brain after the amino acid glutamate. The various functions performed by N-acetyl aspartic acid  are still being studied, but the main functions proposed include (1) acting  as a neuronal osmolyte involved in fluid balance in the brain, (2) serving as a  source of acetate for lipid and myelin synthesis in oligodendrocytes, (3) serving as a precursor for the synthesis of the important neurotransmitter dipeptide N-acetylaspartilglutamate  (NAAG), and (4) play a potential role in energy production from the amino acid glutamate in neuronal mitochondria. High levels of N-acetyl aspartic acid can lead to abnormal neural signals, delayed or stopped intellectual development, and difficulties with general motor skills. When present at sufficiently high levels, N-acetyl aspartic acid can be a neurotoxin, an acidogen and a metabotoxin. This situation manifests itself in case of genetic disorders, such as Canavan's disease, causing brain degeneration.
acidoNacetilaspartico.hight: The slight excess of N-acetyl aspartic acid could result from heavy use of products with aspartate (beverages or soy). If much higher, follow neuronal tests.

acidomalonico.name: Malonic acid
acidomalonico.base: Malonic acid is involved in the biosynthesis of fatty acids, joining with acetic acid for conversion into acetoacetic acid. Malonic acid has been detected in several foods such as red beets, corn, beans and cow's milk. This could make malonic acid a potential biomarker for consumption of these foods. Malonic acid has been found to be associated with several diseases such as eosinophilic esophagitis, combined malonic and methylmalonic aciduria, and early preeclampsia.
acidomalonico.hight: Excess malonic acid could indicate important intake of certain foods or linked to previously reported diseases.

acido4idrossifenilpiruvico.name: 4-Hydroxyphenylpyruvic acid
acido4idrossifenilpiruvico.base: 4-Hydroxyphenylpyruvic acid is a keto acid involved in the tyrosine catabolism pathway, which is then converted to homogentisic acid by the enzyme 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvic acid dioxygenase (HPD). HPD enzyme deficiency causes phenylketonuria, a congenital metabolic error.
acido4idrossifenilpiruvico.hight: The strong excess is related to an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by elevated levels of tyrosine in the blood and massive excretion of tyrosine derivatives in the urine.

acido2idrossi3metilbutirrico.name: 2-Hydroxy-3-methylbutyric acid
acido2idrossi3metilbutirrico.base: 2-Hydroxy-3-methylbutyric acid originates from ketogenesis and the metabolism of valine, leucine and isoleucine. It is present in patients with phenylketonuria, acidemia, maple syrup disease and other congenital metabolic disorders or in cases of ketoacidosis.
acido2idrossi3metilbutirrico.hight: The minimal excess highlights states of lactic acidosis or ketoacidosis. In cases where the levels are much higher than the reference range, these are important congenital pathologies.

creatinina.name: Creatinine
creatinina.base: Creatinine is commonly calculated for the evaluation of urinary analytes. Its excretion is influenced by skeletal muscle and body state.
creatinina.hight: The intake of dietary proteins containing arginine and glycine (precursors of creatinine) and creatinine supplementation itself can increase their levels. The state of hydration also plays an important role. Excess creatinine can also show renal dysfunction.
creatinina.low: Its deficit is usually related to too little muscle mass. A low value could also indicate an increase in glomerular filtration rate (eGFR).
